readings.source: Argus-linked reporting (3 Jul) confirms continued falls; physical indications $450/t (17 Jun) → $420/t (18 Jun). Indicative — exact 6 Jul Egypt assessment is subscription-only.
June Yara–Mosaic contract confirmed at $775/t CFR, down $50/t from May's $825. No July contract agreed as of 3 Jul (monthly benchmark).
CME July settlement $750/st (2 Jul), down from $782.50 (25 Jun), ≈ −4.2% w/w. Aug $740, Sep $737.50.
Public indications ~$405–410/t CFR; a separate series $412.50 (25 Jun). Indicative, not exchange-traded.
Front-month ≈ €44.45/MWh (6 Jul), intraday €44.34–45.49; €42.57 on 29 Jun → ≈ +4.4% w/w (easing from Fri €45.10 close).

editorialReading: The pressure is easing across the hemisphere. Global urea (Egypt FOB ~$420–450/t) has roughly halved from its May spike as the broader ceasefire remains formally in place — though a new tanker strike near Oman has renewed concern over safe passage through Hormuz. June Tampa ammonia settled at $775/t (down $50), and DAP is drifting lower on the CME curve ($750/st July → $737.50 September) — NOLA-benchmarked, so directly relevant to US buyers. The US keeps its structural edge (CF Industries' Donaldsonville and Yazoo City capacity, the Tampa benchmark), but the swing risk sits with import-dependent neighbours: Brazil is buying potash around $405–415/t CFR ahead of the Safrinha, and Mexico leans on Gulf nitrogen flows. The one line still rising is TTF gas (~€44.5/MWh, +4.4% w/w) — a European cost driver, but a reminder that the nitrogen complex's cost floor is energy, not the war premium that is now unwinding.
watchNext: Whether urea extends its slide toward the pre-war ~$390–420/t area, and whether a July Tampa ammonia contract settles below June's $775/t — the Western-Hemisphere benchmark. Watch the CME DAP / urea curve for the US buy-side signal, Brazilian potash CFR into the Safrinha, and TTF gas as the global nitrogen cost floor. The squeeze on hemisphere food security runs less through US farmer prices than through import-dependent neighbours' access to affordable nitrogen. Sourcing note: real-time Argus / Green Markets assessments are subscription-only and the free World Bank Pink Sheet lags ~a month — these are publicly-cited indications, and the urea figure in particular is indicative, not a confirmed 6 July assessment.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
